title: A Dream
content:
آ آ آ آ Was it a dream? We sailï؟½d, I thought we sailï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Martin and I, down a green Alpine stream,
آ آ آ آ Under oï؟½erhanging pines; the morning sun,
آ آ آ آ On the wet umbrage of their glossy tops,
آ آ آ آ On the red pinings of their forest floor,
آ آ آ آ Drew a warm scent abroad; behind the pines
آ آ آ آ The mountain skirts, with all their sylvan change
آ آ آ آ Of bright-leafï؟½d chestnuts, and mossï؟½d walnut-trees,
آ آ آ آ And the frail scarlet-berried ash, began.
آ آ آ آ Swiss chalets glitterï؟½d on the dewy slopes,
آ آ آ آ And from some swarded shelf high up, there came
آ آ آ آ Notes of wild pastoral music: over all
آ آ آ آ Rangï؟½d, diamond-bright, the eternal wall of snow.
آ آ آ آ Upon the mossy rocks at the streamï؟½s edge.
آ آ آ آ Backï؟½d by the pines, a plank-built cottage stood,
آ آ آ آ Bright in the sun; the climbing gourd-plantï؟½s leaves
آ آ آ آ Muffled its walls, and on the stone-strewn roof
آ آ آ آ Lay the warm golden gourds; golden, within,
آ آ آ آ Under the eaves, peerï؟½d rows of Indian corn.
آ آ آ آ We shot beneath the cottage with the stream.
آ آ آ آ On the brown rude-carvï؟½d balcony two Forms
آ آ آ آ Came forth, Oliviaï؟½s, Marguerite! and thine.
آ آ آ آ Clad were they both in white, flowers in their breast;
آ آ آ آ Straw hats bedeckï؟½d their heads, with ribbons blue
آ آ آ آ Which wavï؟½d, and on their shoulders fluttering playï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ They saw us, they conferrï؟½d; their bosoms heavï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And more than mortal impulse fillï؟½d their eyes.
آ آ آ آ Their lips movï؟½d; their white arms, wavï؟½d eagerly,
آ آ آ آ Flashï؟½d once, like falling streams:, we rose, we gazï؟½d
آ آ آ آ One moment, on the rapidï؟½s top, our boat
آ آ آ آ Hung poisï؟½d, and then the darting River of Life,
آ آ آ آ Loud thundering, bore us by: swift, swift it foamï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ Black under cliffs it racï؟½d, round headlands shone.
آ آ آ آ Soon the plankï؟½d cottage ï؟½mid the sun-warmï؟½d pines
آ آ آ آ Faded, the moss, the rocks; us burning Plains
آ آ آ آ Bristled with cities, us the Sea receivï؟½d
meaning:
About: Matthew Arnold (24 December 1822 ï؟½ 15 April 1888) was an English poet and cultural critic, who worked as an inspector of schools. 
He was the son of Thomas Arnold, the famed headmaster of Rugby School, and brother to Tom Arnold, literary professor, and William Delafield Arnold, novelist and colonial administrator.
